title: Vomit on Car Upholstery
stainChemistry: Vomit is a complex mixture of partially digested food, stomach acid (HCl, pH 1.5-3.5), bile salts, mucus, and proteins. On car upholstery, the acid can damage dyes and fibers if left in contact, while the protein component bonds and sets with heat. Bile salts act as surfactants that drive the stain deeper into the substrate. The odor comes from volatile fatty acids and bacterial decomposition.
whyThisWorks: Immediate removal of solids prevents acid damage to car upholstery. Cold water dilutes the acid and prevents protein from setting. Enzyme treatment breaks down the protein matrix, while surfactant addresses the bile and fat components. Odor neutralization requires reaching all contaminated material beneath the surface.
defaultAssumption: Assume stain age is unknown and possibly heat-set unless user specifies otherwise. Start conservative.

steps: 1. Apply Protein Formula (digestant spotter) and tamp gently with spotting brush. Work from edge to center
2. Apply NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent) and work gently to remove loosened protein and digestant residue. Flush with cold water
3. If stain persists: apply sodium hydrosulfite (reducing agent) for stubborn oxidized protein — 5-10 min dwell time, monitor carefully
4. Apply Acetic Acid 28% (diluted 1:10) as neutralizing rinse to restore fabric pH
5. Blot dry with clean absorbent cloth. Air dry — no heat
warnings: COLD water only throughout — heat permanently sets protein
Avoid over-wetting — extract excess moisture to prevent backing/foam damage

steps: 1. Remove solids with a spoon or dull edge — scrape, don't rub. Dispose safely
2. Blot area with cold water and clean white cloths to dilute acid
3. Mix 1 tbsp Dawn dish soap with 2 cups cold water. Apply and blot
4. Mix equal parts white vinegar and cold water. Apply for odor neutralization
5. Blot with clean white cloth until dry. Sprinkle baking soda over area — let sit 15 min
6. Vacuum and air dry — no heat
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop and take to a professional if the stain has been heat-set, if multiple home treatments haven't improved it, or if the fabric is delicate/expensive
warnings: NEVER use hot water on protein stains — heat sets them permanently
Always test cleaning solution on a hidden area first

neverDo: No chlorine bleach — damages automotive fabric
No excess water — soaks into seat foam and causes mold/mildew
fiberSensitivities: Automotive fabrics are usually synthetic (nylon/polyester) — chemically durable
Seat foam absorbs moisture — use minimum liquid, extract thoroughly
Test in hidden area (under seat) before visible treatment

escalation.when: If the protein has been heat-set (hot water, dryer, or iron exposure), or if the stain is older than 48 hours without treatment, or if 3 treatment cycles haven't resolved it
escalation.whatToTell: Explain that the stain is vomit on car upholstery, how old it is, and what products were already used. This helps the professional choose the right chemistry without duplicating failed approaches

contextQuestions: Has it been exposed to heat (dryer, iron, hot water)?
Have you already tried treating it? If so, with what products?
Is the stain fresh and wet, or dried/crusty?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Stain age and heat exposure are the two biggest factors. Fresh stains with no heat exposure have the highest success rate. Heat-set or aged stains may require professional treatment.
